Name: root_shooting_mcts
Title: Root-Shooting MCTS: Best-Arm-First Simulation Allocation for Low-Budget Planning
Short Hypothesis: When per-decision simulation budgets are very small, the key failure mode of vanilla UCT is not insufficient deep exploration everywhere, but misidentifying the best root action due to spreading simulations too thinly across the tree. A root-focused MCTS variant that explicitly treats action choice at the current state as a best-arm identification problem, while using shallow subtree probes only to refine promising root actions, should improve simple regret and final decision quality on deterministic synthetic planning tasks. This direction is needed because the benchmark evaluates one action choice at a time under tight budgets; deeper asymptotic tree balance is less relevant than reliably selecting the next action now. Simpler alternatives such as tuning the UCT constant do not directly change the objective from cumulative-regret-style exploration to low-budget root decision accuracy.
Related Work: UCT and standard MCTS optimize a generic exploration-exploitation tradeoff but are known to be brittle under small budgets because they allocate effort throughout the tree rather than prioritizing root action identification. Some prior work connects MCTS to simple-regret or best-arm ideas, and recent perturbation-based best-arm identification for MCTS shows that root-action identification can matter in planning. There is also limited prior work combining sequential halving with UCT. This proposal is distinct in three ways: (1) it is a simple, named MCTS variant tailored to fixed per-decision budgets in deterministic synthetic planning; (2) it uses an explicit two-phase simulation allocation rule at every decision step—successive elimination over root actions followed by local UCT probing only within surviving actions; and (3) it studies whether this root-shooting allocation is especially effective in small combinatorial planning tasks, rather than robotics TAMP or large-scale domains. The contribution is therefore not a trivial retuning of UCT, but a change in the objective and budget allocation structure of MCTS.
Abstract: We propose Root-Shooting MCTS (RS-MCTS), a budget-efficient Monte Carlo Tree Search variant designed for settings where only a small fixed number of simulations can be spent before each action. Vanilla UCT often underperforms in this regime because it distributes simulations across many interior nodes, despite the immediate planning objective being to identify the best root action. RS-MCTS reframes each planning step as a root best-arm identification problem with structured subtree probing. Given budget B and root branching factor K, RS-MCTS first allocates simulations in rounds across all root actions. In each round, every surviving root action receives an equal number of subtree probes, where each probe performs one-step commitment to that root action followed by a shallow UCT search or heuristic rollout inside its subtree. After each round, poorly performing root actions are eliminated using empirical lower-confidence ranking, similar in spirit to successive halving. The remaining budget is then concentrated on the surviving root actions, with the final action chosen by the highest estimated value. This design preserves the strengths of MCTS inside each candidate subtree while preventing early budget waste on globally uncompetitive actions. We will evaluate RS-MCTS on deterministic grid reward collection, graph orienteering, and deadline scheduling under fixed simulation budgets, comparing against vanilla UCT, greedy search, beam search, and ablations. We hypothesize that explicit root-focused allocation will substantially reduce simple regret and improve return in the low-budget regime, yielding a practical and theoretically motivated MCTS variant for budgeted planning.
Experiments: Implement Root-Shooting MCTS with the following rule: at a root state with actions A={a1,...,aK} and total budget B, maintain a survivor set S=A. While |S|>1 and budget remains, allocate m=ceil(B_remaining/(|S|*ceil(log2 K)+1)) probes to each a in S. A probe commits to root action a, transitions to the child state, then spends a small fixed internal budget b_sub on a local planner inside that subtree (either shallow UCT or heuristic rollout). Update each root action estimate Q(a) from probe returns. Eliminate the bottom half of S by empirical mean or lower confidence bound. Use leftover budget to probe the final survivors and choose argmax_a Q(a).

Compare RS-MCTS against unchanged vanilla UCT/MCTS, random, greedy, and beam-search baselines under identical per-decision budgets, e.g. {8, 16, 32, 64, 128} simulations. Report average episodic return, regret to the best observed solution, optimality gap where exact optimum is available, and win rate versus vanilla UCT across seeds.

Run an ablation on the internal probe mechanism: (i) pure random rollout after root commitment, (ii) heuristic rollout, (iii) shallow UCT with depth cap, to test whether the gain comes from root allocation rather than stronger rollouts alone.

Run an ablation on elimination strategy: halve-by-mean, halve-by-lower-confidence-bound, and no elimination (uniform root allocation). This isolates whether successive elimination is the key component.

Measure budget utilization and search shape statistics: fraction of simulations spent distinguishing root actions, average depth reached, number of unique root actions sampled, and subtree concentration. Correlate these with performance to support the mechanism claim.

Stress-test on environments with different branching factors and horizon lengths by varying grid action availability, graph degree, and task counts. Hypothesis: RS-MCTS helps most when root branching is moderate and budgets are too small for full-tree balancing.

Optionally compare against a root-only simple-regret baseline such as flat Monte Carlo evaluation of each root action without tree search, showing that RS-MCTS benefits from combining best-arm allocation with local subtree search rather than either ingredient alone.
Risk Factors and Limitations: If the benchmark tasks require deep lookahead to distinguish actions, over-focusing on the root may miss crucial deeper structure and underperform vanilla UCT at larger budgets.

Successive elimination may be brittle when early probe estimates are noisy; a truly good root action could be discarded too early. Conservative elimination thresholds or confidence bounds may be needed.
The method may help less when root branching factor is tiny, since vanilla UCT already allocates enough attention to each action under small K.

Because the environments are deterministic, gains might partly come from reduced variance rather than a generally superior allocation principle; external validity to stochastic domains would remain untested.
A strong heuristic rollout inside probes could confound the contribution, so careful ablations are necessary to show that root-focused allocation itself drives improvements.

Theoretical novelty may be moderate if reviewers view the method as an adaptation of sequential halving to MCTS; the paper must emphasize the concrete low-budget planning setting, algorithmic integration with subtree search, and empirical insights across domains.

Name: disagreement_triggered_mcts
Title: Disagreement-Triggered MCTS: Spend Simulations Only Where Value Proxies Conflict
Short Hypothesis: In very small per-decision budgets, vanilla UCT wastes simulations by repeatedly refining branches whose ranking is already obvious while neglecting the few states where cheap value proxies disagree and the decision is genuinely ambiguous. We hypothesize that a budget-efficient MCTS variant should treat search as targeted ambiguity resolution: use multiple ultra-cheap proxy evaluators at each node, and invoke expensive subtree simulation only when those proxies disagree beyond a threshold. This is the right setting because the benchmark tasks are deterministic, structured, and small enough that simple handcrafted proxies can be computed cheaply but still be imperfect; thus disagreement between proxies is an informative signal of where search is needed. Simpler alternatives like tuning exploration constants or using a single better rollout policy do not explicitly identify and concentrate budget on ambiguous decisions.
Related Work: Prior low-budget MCTS work mainly reallocates simulations via modified UCB rules, root-focused best-arm identification, progressive widening, or stronger rollouts/value estimates. There is also literature on selective tree expansion, metareasoning, and uncertainty-aware search, but typically uncertainty is estimated from Monte Carlo variance or learned value models. This proposal is distinct in using disagreement among multiple deterministic, hand-computable proxy evaluators as the trigger for whether to search deeper, expand, or stop. The method does not merely replace random rollouts with heuristics; it uses a committee of cheap proxies to decide where simulation is worth spending. It is also not a trivial ensemble heuristic planner, because the proxies are embedded into MCTS control flow: selection bonuses, expansion gating, rollout termination, and backup confidence all depend on proxy disagreement. This makes the contribution a novel budget-allocation principle for low-budget MCTS on synthetic planning tasks.
Abstract: We propose Disagreement-Triggered MCTS (DT-MCTS), a Monte Carlo Tree Search variant designed for fixed, small simulation budgets. The core idea is simple: simulations should be spent only on states whose action ranking is ambiguous under cheap value proxies. At each encountered state, DT-MCTS computes a small committee of deterministic proxy evaluations for each action, such as myopic reward gain, greedy completion value, and a lightweight optimistic bound based on remaining resources. The dispersion of these proxy rankings defines a disagreement score. Selection then prioritizes actions with high predicted value and high disagreement, while expansion and rollout are gated by disagreement thresholds: if proxies strongly agree on the best continuation, DT-MCTS truncates search and backs up the proxy consensus; if they disagree, the algorithm allocates deeper simulations to resolve the ambiguity. Backups maintain both empirical return and disagreement-reduced confidence, so nodes that become proxy-consistent stop consuming budget. This reframes low-budget MCTS as selective deliberation rather than uniform stochastic estimation. The approach is especially suitable for deterministic synthetic planning domains, where cheap proxies can exploit domain structure yet remain imperfect enough that selective search matters. We will evaluate DT-MCTS on grid reward collection, graph orienteering, and deadline scheduling under matched per-decision budgets against vanilla UCT, greedy, beam search, and ablations. We hypothesize that disagreement-triggered simulation allocation will improve average return and reduce regret by concentrating computation on the few decisions where search truly changes the chosen action.
Experiments: Implement DT-MCTS with a committee of 3 cheap proxy evaluators available in all domains: (1) one-step immediate reward plus residual heuristic, (2) greedy completion heuristic from the current state, and (3) optimistic bound using remaining horizon/budget and top reachable rewards or tasks. For each node s and action a, compute proxy scores v_j(s,a). Define disagreement D(s)=mean pairwise rank disagreement across actions or standard deviation of normalized proxy scores for the top actions.

Modify selection from UCT to: score(s,a)=Q(s,a)+c*sqrt(log N(s)/(N(s,a)+1))+lambda*D(child(s,a))/sqrt(N(s,a)+1). Intuition: ambiguous children get extra attention only early, and the bonus decays with visits. Compare against vanilla UCT under identical simulation budgets.

Add disagreement-triggered expansion/termination: after taking action a to child s', if D(s')<tau_low and all proxies agree on the top action margin > m, stop simulation early and return proxy-consensus value V_proxy(s'); if D(s')>tau_high, expand and continue tree search; otherwise perform a short heuristic rollout of capped depth d. This changes simulation allocation, not just evaluation.

Use disagreement-aware backup: each simulation returns both reward G and average disagreement encountered. Backup Q as usual, but maintain node confidence C(s,a)=1/(1+avg_disagreement_after_a). Final root choice is argmax_a [Q(s,a)+beta*C(s,a)] or simply highest Q after DT allocation. Ablate whether confidence should influence only allocation or also final action choice.

Benchmark on the three provided environments with per-decision budgets {4, 8, 16, 32, 64, 128}. Report average episodic return, regret to best observed solution, optimality gap where available, and area-under-budget-curve to emphasize low-budget quality.
Ablation 1: single-proxy versions versus 3-proxy committee. This tests whether gains come from better heuristics alone or from disagreement as a signal.
Ablation 2: disagreement used only for early rollout stopping, only for selection bonus, only for expansion gating, and full DT-MCTS. This isolates which control point matters most.

Ablation 3: replace disagreement signal with Monte Carlo empirical variance or visit-count uncertainty while keeping the rest of the algorithm fixed. This tests whether proxy disagreement is a better low-budget ambiguity detector than sample variance.

Analyze mechanism by logging fraction of simulations terminated early, average depth, number of expanded nodes, and how often the final chosen action differs from the top proxy action. Correlate performance gains with concentration of budget on high-disagreement states.

Stress-test robustness by intentionally degrading one proxy (e.g., noisy or myopic heuristic) and measuring whether committee disagreement still helps or becomes misleading. Also vary branching factor and horizon in the synthetic tasks to identify regimes where DT-MCTS is strongest.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The method depends on designing cheap proxies that are informative yet imperfect; if proxies are too weak or too correlated, disagreement may not meaningfully identify ambiguous states.

Reviewers may argue the approach is domain-engineered because proxy definitions exploit task structure. The paper must emphasize that the committee uses simple generic planning heuristics available across all benchmark domains.
If proxy disagreement is poorly calibrated, DT-MCTS could terminate search too early on difficult states or over-search states with misleading proxy conflict.
The approach may provide less benefit at large budgets, where vanilla UCT eventually corrects poor early allocation and proxy gating becomes unnecessary.
Because the environments are deterministic and synthetic, the utility of disagreement-triggered search in stochastic or learned-model settings would remain an open question.
Implementation requires careful normalization of proxy scores across domains so disagreement is comparable; otherwise thresholds may be brittle.

If one proxy dominates the others, the method can collapse into heuristic search. Strong ablations are needed to show that selective simulation allocation, not just heuristic quality, drives the gains.

Name: quantile_guarded_mcts
Title: Quantile-Guarded MCTS: Conservative Backups for Low-Budget Deterministic Planning
Short Hypothesis: Under very small per-decision simulation budgets, vanilla UCT often fails not because it cannot explore enough root actions, but because its mean backup rule is too eager: a single lucky rollout through a poorly sampled branch can dominate action selection and propagate deep noise upward. We hypothesize that in deterministic synthetic planning tasks, replacing mean-only backups with a visit-adaptive conservative backup that interpolates between lower-quantile, trimmed-mean, and standard mean estimates will improve final decision quality by suppressing fragile high estimates until enough evidence accumulates. This is the right setting because the benchmark fixes tiny budgets and uses deterministic combinatorial tasks where rollout-induced estimator noise, not transition stochasticity, is the main issue; thus backup robustness can be isolated cleanly. Simpler alternatives like changing only the exploration constant or rollout policy do not directly address the core failure mode of overconfident value propagation from undersampled nodes.
Related Work: Classical UCT uses sample-mean backups, which are statistically efficient asymptotically but can be brittle in low-sample regimes. Prior MCTS work has modified selection, root allocation, rollout policies, and sometimes backup operators using max, mixmax, implicit minimax, or variance-aware estimates, mostly in adversarial games or stochastic settings. There is also literature on robust statistics such as trimmed means and quantile estimators, but these ideas have not been cleanly developed as a budget-aware backup principle for deterministic single-agent planning benchmarks. This proposal is distinct in three ways: (1) it targets the underexplored backup rule itself as the primary lever for low-budget improvement, rather than root allocation or heuristic gating; (2) it introduces a named, visit-adaptive robust backup specifically designed to be conservative early and asymptotically recover standard mean backups later; and (3) it evaluates this backup-centric intervention across small synthetic planning domains where confounds from learned models, stochasticity, or huge compute are absent. The result is not a trivial extension of UCT, but a new low-budget MCTS objective: prefer actions whose value is supported robustly, not merely optimistically, when evidence is scarce.
Abstract: We propose Quantile-Guarded MCTS (QG-MCTS), a budget-efficient Monte Carlo Tree Search variant for low-budget deterministic planning. The key observation is that vanilla UCT backs up sample means too aggressively: with only a few simulations, one unusually good rollout can inflate a branch’s value and redirect much of the remaining budget toward a fragile estimate. QG-MCTS replaces this backup rule with a conservative, visit-adaptive robust estimator. For each action node, we store the multiset of observed rollout returns and compute a guarded value estimate that is pessimistic when the visit count is small and gradually approaches the sample mean as evidence accumulates. Concretely, the backup can combine a lower empirical quantile, a trimmed mean, and the ordinary mean using visit-dependent weights. Selection then uses this guarded estimate inside UCT, preserving the standard exploration bonus while changing what is considered the current value of an action. The intuition is simple: under tight budgets, the planner should trust branches whose good performance is repeatable, not branches supported by a single outlier trajectory. Because the benchmark domains are deterministic, any instability arises from search allocation and rollout truncation rather than environmental randomness, making them ideal for isolating the effect of robust backups. We will evaluate QG-MCTS on grid reward collection, graph orienteering, and deadline scheduling under fixed per-decision budgets against vanilla UCT, beam search, greedy planning, and backup ablations. We hypothesize that conservative early backups reduce simple regret, improve average return, and produce more stable action choices across seeds, especially at budgets where vanilla UCT is most brittle.
Experiments: Implement Quantile-Guarded MCTS by modifying only the backup/value-estimation component. For each edge (s,a), maintain observed returns R_{s,a}={r_1,...,r_n}. Define the guarded estimate as Q_guard(s,a)=w_n * Q_q(s,a) + u_n * Q_trim(s,a) + (1-w_n-u_n) * Q_mean(s,a), where Q_q is the empirical lower quantile (e.g. q=0.25), Q_trim is the alpha-trimmed mean (e.g. trim 20% on each side when enough samples exist), and Q_mean is the ordinary sample mean. Use visit-adaptive weights such as w_n=min(1,c1/sqrt(n)), u_n=min(1-w_n,c2/sqrt(n)), so the estimator is conservative for small n and converges toward the mean for large n.

Use standard UCT selection but replace Q with Q_guard: select argmax_a [Q_guard(s,a) + c * sqrt(log N(s) / (N(s,a)+1))]. Keep expansion and rollout identical to the vanilla baseline to isolate the backup contribution. Final root action is chosen by highest Q_guard or, in an ablation, highest Q_mean after search using guarded allocation.

Compare against unchanged vanilla UCT/MCTS, random, greedy, and beam search under identical per-decision budgets, e.g. {4, 8, 16, 32, 64, 128}. Report average episodic return, regret to the best observed solution, optimality gap where exact optimum is available, and area under the performance-vs-budget curve to emphasize low-budget quality.

Run backup-rule ablations: (i) pure mean backup, (ii) pure lower-quantile backup, (iii) pure trimmed-mean backup, (iv) static convex combination, and (v) full visit-adaptive QG-MCTS. This tests whether adaptation across visit counts matters beyond any single robust statistic.

Run a root-decision stability analysis across random seeds and rollout randomness: measure how often the chosen root action changes across repeated runs from the same state and budget. Hypothesis: QG-MCTS should show lower action instability than vanilla UCT because it is less sensitive to early outlier returns.

Measure overcommitment behavior by logging, for each planner, the fraction of budget spent on the ultimately chosen root branch after that branch’s first high-return rollout. Hypothesis: vanilla UCT will over-concentrate after lucky early samples, while QG-MCTS will delay commitment until repeated evidence appears.

Evaluate interaction with rollout quality by testing both random rollouts and a simple heuristic rollout policy. If QG-MCTS still helps under both, this supports the claim that the gain comes from backup robustness rather than merely compensating for weak rollouts.

Stress-test on synthetic variants with sparse rewards versus dense rewards, and short versus long horizons. Hypothesis: guarded backups help most in sparse-reward, long-horizon settings where occasional lucky completions create extreme return outliers.

Optional algorithmic variant: depth-aware guarding, where deeper nodes use stronger conservatism because rollout noise compounds with depth. Compare depth-aware versus depth-agnostic guarding to test whether backup robustness should depend on remaining horizon.
Risk Factors and Limitations: If rollout returns are not actually heavy-tailed or outlier-prone in these benchmarks, robust backups may become unnecessarily pessimistic and slow down identification of genuinely strong actions.
Maintaining return samples for quantiles and trimmed means adds some bookkeeping overhead, though this should remain modest in the small-budget academic setting.

A too-conservative backup may underexplore rare but truly optimal branches, especially in sparse-reward tasks where early success signals are essential. Careful calibration of visit-adaptive weights is required.

Because the tasks are deterministic, reviewers may question whether the method generalizes to stochastic domains; the paper should frame the contribution as low-budget planning under search-induced uncertainty, not environmental uncertainty.

Theoretical analysis may be harder than for standard UCT because the estimator is biased early by design. The paper may need to emphasize empirical gains and intuitive robustness rather than strong regret guarantees.

If the benchmark’s vanilla implementation already uses very shallow rollouts or deterministic heuristics, return distributions may be too narrow for quantile guarding to matter much. In that case, stronger stress tests with sparse-reward settings will be important.

Name: frontier_recycling_mcts
Title: Frontier-Recycling MCTS: Reusing Partial Search Evidence Across Consecutive Decisions Under Tiny Budgets
Short Hypothesis: In fixed small per-decision budget settings, vanilla UCT wastes a large fraction of computation by rebuilding a fresh tree after every executed action, even though consecutive decision states in deterministic planning tasks are highly correlated. We hypothesize that the best low-budget intervention is not only to change within-decision allocation, but to recycle and selectively repair the previously explored frontier across time steps. In the provided synthetic domains, executing one action deterministically moves the agent to a child state whose unexplored siblings, rollout suffixes, and local action rankings remain informative for nearby future states. A named MCTS variant that carries forward a compact frontier cache, reanchors it at the new root, and spends the next tiny budget primarily on inconsistency repair rather than full re-search should improve return and regret. This is the right setting because the benchmark repeatedly invokes planning under tiny budgets in deterministic environments with structured state overlap; simpler alternatives like tuning UCT or changing only the rollout policy cannot exploit this cross-decision redundancy.
Related Work: Classical UCT typically reuses only the chosen subtree after an action, discarding most of the surrounding search effort. There is prior work on tree reuse in game MCTS, transposition tables, and persistent search trees, but these are usually designed for large searches, adversarial games, or asymptotic efficiency rather than tiny per-decision budgets in deterministic single-agent planning. There is also work on real-time search and LRTA*-style heuristic updates, but that literature does not study Monte Carlo budget allocation and rollout-based value repair. This proposal is distinct in three ways: (1) it treats cross-decision reuse itself as the main low-budget lever, rather than modifying only selection or backups inside a single planning call; (2) it introduces a concrete frontier-recycling mechanism that preserves not just the chosen subtree but a compressed set of nearby frontier statistics and candidate continuations from discarded branches; and (3) it evaluates whether selective repair of stale statistics can outperform fresh-search UCT on small synthetic planning tasks. Thus the contribution is not a trivial persistent-tree implementation, but a new budget-aware planning principle: spend new simulations correcting old search evidence instead of repeatedly rediscovering it.
Abstract: We propose Frontier-Recycling MCTS (FR-MCTS), a budget-efficient tree-search variant for deterministic planning with very small per-decision simulation budgets. Standard MCTS plans myopically one decision at a time: after executing an action, most search effort from the previous step is discarded, and the next planning call rebuilds local evidence from scratch. This is especially wasteful in deterministic combinatorial tasks where consecutive states are strongly related and planning budgets are too small to repeatedly reconstruct useful action rankings. FR-MCTS addresses this by carrying forward a compact cache of frontier evidence across decisions. After each planning step, the algorithm retains not only the chosen child subtree, but also a bounded set of high-value frontier nodes, rollout suffix statistics, and action-priority summaries from nearby branches. At the next state, FR-MCTS reanchors any cache entries consistent with the new root, initializes node priors and visit counts from recycled evidence, and allocates the new budget primarily to repair operations: revalidate stale estimates, expand only where cached rankings conflict with current local heuristics, and otherwise exploit inherited structure. This converts per-decision planning from repeated cold-start search into warm-start search with selective inconsistency correction. We will evaluate FR-MCTS on grid reward collection, graph orienteering, and deadline scheduling under fixed simulation budgets against vanilla UCT, greedy, beam search, and ablations for subtree-only reuse and cache repair. We hypothesize that cross-decision frontier recycling will yield substantial gains in the low-budget regime by amortizing search effort over the whole episode, improving average return, reducing regret, and producing more stable action choices without requiring additional compute or learned models.
Experiments: Implement FR-MCTS with three components. (1) Standard within-step UCT search for budget B. (2) After selecting and executing root action a_t, retain the chosen subtree as usual, but also store a bounded frontier cache C of up to M entries from non-chosen but informative nodes encountered during search. Each cache entry contains a canonical state key, depth-to-go, empirical value estimate Q, visit count N, best-action ranking, and optional rollout suffix return statistics. Keep entries with high utility score U_cache = Q + alpha/sqrt(N+1) + gamma * novelty, where novelty is whether the state was rarely revisited. (3) At the next decision state s_{t+1}, before running simulations, reanchor all cache entries whose state key matches or can be deterministically advanced to descendants of s_{t+1}; use them to initialize node priors: N0(s,a)=rho*N_cached(s,a), Q0(s,a)=Q_cached(s,a). Then spend the new budget on repair-first UCT: for any initialized node, force one repair simulation before trusting inherited values; after repair, continue standard UCT with inherited statistics.

Compare FR-MCTS against unchanged vanilla UCT/MCTS, random, greedy, and beam search under identical per-decision budgets such as {4, 8, 16, 32, 64}. Report average episodic return, regret to best observed solution, optimality gap where available, and area under the performance-versus-budget curve.

Ablation 1: subtree-only reuse versus full frontier recycling. Subtree-only reuse keeps only the chosen child subtree, matching standard persistent-tree practice. Full FR-MCTS additionally keeps off-path frontier entries. This isolates whether off-path evidence is the novel source of gains.
Ablation 2: inherited priors without repair versus inherited priors with mandatory repair. This tests whether stale statistics help or hurt when not corrected.
Ablation 3: cache content types: (i) state-action visit/value statistics only, (ii) rollout suffix statistics only, (iii) both. This identifies which recycled evidence matters most.

Ablation 4: cache replacement rules. Compare top-Q retention, uncertainty-weighted retention, and diversity-aware retention based on state dissimilarity. Evaluate whether diversity in the recycled frontier is important under tiny budgets.

Measure search-efficiency diagnostics: fraction of simulations spent on previously seen states, average effective depth reached, number of newly expanded nodes per decision, cache hit rate, and value drift between cached and repaired estimates. Hypothesis: FR-MCTS should achieve higher cache hit rates and fewer cold expansions while maintaining or improving returns.

Stress-test by varying horizon length and branching factor in each synthetic domain. Hypothesis: frontier recycling helps most when episodes are multi-step and local decisions are strongly coupled, but may help less in very short-horizon tasks where there is little future reuse.

Run a fairness control where total episode-level simulation count is matched. Compare vanilla UCT with budget B per decision to FR-MCTS with the same B, showing that gains come from amortized reuse rather than hidden extra compute.

Optional negative-control experiment: randomize state keys before cache lookup, which preserves overhead but destroys meaningful reuse. Performance should collapse toward vanilla UCT if the mechanism is truly exploiting cross-decision structure.
Risk Factors and Limitations: If the benchmark implementation already preserves the chosen subtree across actions, the incremental novelty must come clearly from off-path frontier recycling and repair, not generic tree persistence.

The synthetic tasks may have limited exact state revisitation, reducing cache hit rates. The method therefore depends on using canonical state abstractions or descendant-consistency checks that are informative but still cheap.
Inherited statistics can be stale after an action changes remaining resources or horizon; without careful repair, FR-MCTS may overtrust outdated values and make systematically biased decisions.
Benefits may be modest in one-shot or very short-horizon settings where there is little opportunity to amortize search across time.
State-key design may introduce some domain engineering. The paper should emphasize generic canonicalization based on the provided SearchProblem state representation rather than handcrafted features.

Reviewers may view this as an engineering optimization unless the experiments clearly show a new low-budget phenomenon: cross-decision amortization dominates within-decision exploration tweaks when budgets are tiny.
Memory grows with cache size, though this remains easily affordable in an academic lab and can be controlled with a small fixed-capacity cache.

Name: depth_staged_mcts
Title: Depth-Staged MCTS: Allocate Tiny Budgets by Solving Shallow Decisions Before Deep Ones
Short Hypothesis: When the per-decision simulation budget is very small, vanilla UCT spends too many rollouts traversing deep and weakly informed parts of the tree before it has reliably ranked the first few actions that actually determine the near-term trajectory. We hypothesize that a simple staged variant of MCTS that enforces a depth-dependent simulation schedule—first spending budget to stabilize value estimates up to depth 1, then depth 2, and only later allowing unrestricted deeper search—will outperform vanilla UCT on deterministic synthetic planning tasks. This is the right setting because the tasks are sequential single-agent problems with short-to-moderate horizons and fixed tiny budgets, so early action errors dominate outcome quality. Existing root simple-regret methods and sequential-halving-at-root ideas do not address the broader question of how to ration budget across depths after the root decision itself.
Related Work: Prior work has noted that UCT’s cumulative-regret style allocation can be suboptimal for final action selection, especially at the root. Pepels et al. (2014) study minimizing simple versus cumulative regret in MCTS, and more recent work such as Anytime Sequential Halving in MCTS (Sagers et al., 2024) revisits root-focused best-arm identification. These methods mainly change root selection or root allocation. Our proposal is clearly different: it introduces a depth-wise budget allocation principle for the whole search process, not only the root. It is also not a trivial depth limit or beam search hybrid. The key novelty is a staged unlocking rule: deeper nodes may only consume substantial budget after shallower layers have received enough balanced evidence. This targets a distinct low-budget failure mode of UCT—premature deep chasing of noisy branches—even when root simple-regret is partially addressed.
Abstract: We propose Depth-Staged MCTS (DS-MCTS), a budget-efficient variant of Monte Carlo Tree Search for deterministic planning under very small per-decision simulation budgets. The core idea is to treat planning as a sequence of increasingly deep evidence-allocation problems rather than allowing unrestricted tree growth from the beginning. Vanilla UCT may waste a large fraction of a tiny budget exploring deep descendants of branches whose shallow action values are still highly uncertain. DS-MCTS addresses this by introducing staged depth unlocking. Given simulation budget B, the algorithm maintains an active depth d_active. During selection, nodes deeper than d_active are evaluated by a cheap rollout or heuristic leaf estimate but are not expanded further. The active depth increases only when the current frontier up to depth d_active has achieved a minimum visit coverage criterion, such as each expanded action at depths less than d_active receiving at least m visits or the root action ranking stabilizing for r consecutive checks. This creates a simple curriculum over search depth: first separate obviously bad shallow choices, then refine promising continuations, and only then invest in deeper lookahead. DS-MCTS preserves the standard MCTS structure—selection, expansion, simulation, and backup—but changes when deeper expansion is allowed. We will evaluate DS-MCTS on grid reward collection, graph orienteering, and deadline scheduling under fixed budgets, comparing against vanilla UCT, greedy planning, beam search, and depth-related ablations. We hypothesize that disciplined depth allocation will improve average return and reduce regret in the low-budget regime by preventing premature commitment to deep but weakly supported branches.
Experiments: Implement DS-MCTS with the following rule. Maintain active depth d_active initialized to 1. During a simulation, use standard UCT selection only while depth <= d_active. If the trajectory reaches depth d_active and the node is nonterminal, stop expansion and return a rollout value V_leaf from either a random rollout or a simple heuristic completion. After every k simulations, check whether the coverage criterion is met: for every expanded node at depths < d_active, each expanded action has at least m(d_active) visits, or alternatively the top-2 root action gap has remained unchanged for r checks. If yes, increment d_active by 1. Continue until budget is exhausted. Final action is the root action with highest empirical value.

Compare DS-MCTS against unchanged vanilla UCT/MCTS, random, greedy, and beam search under identical per-decision budgets such as {4, 8, 16, 32, 64, 128}. Report average episodic return, regret to the best observed solution, optimality gap where available, and area under the performance-vs-budget curve.

Ablation 1: unlocking criteria. Compare visit-coverage unlocking, root-ranking-stability unlocking, and a fixed schedule where d_active increases after preset fractions of the budget (for example 50%, 75%, 90%). This tests whether adaptive staging matters.

Ablation 2: leaf evaluation at the active frontier. Compare random rollout, heuristic rollout, and one-step greedy completion from the frontier. This checks that gains are not solely due to a stronger evaluator.

Ablation 3: stage granularity. Compare DS-MCTS with only root-first staging, full depth-by-depth staging, and a hard depth cap baseline. This isolates whether progressive unlocking across multiple depths is the key contribution.

Mechanism analysis: log how many simulations are spent at each active depth, average expanded depth, number of expanded nodes, and how often the chosen root action changes over the course of the budget. Hypothesis: DS-MCTS should spend more early simulations distinguishing shallow decisions and show more stable root choices.

Stress-test by varying horizon length and branching factor in the synthetic environments. Hypothesis: DS-MCTS should help most when horizons are moderate and branching is large enough that unrestricted deep search is wasteful under tiny budgets.
Optional comparison to a root-simple-regret baseline inspired by sequential halving at the root. This clarifies that depth staging provides benefits beyond root-only allocation.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The method may underperform when optimal decisions depend on rare deep interactions that cannot be inferred from shallow evidence, especially at slightly larger budgets where unrestricted UCT can exploit deep search better.
If the environments have very short horizons or tiny branching factors, staged depth control may offer little advantage over vanilla UCT.
A poorly chosen unlocking criterion could be too conservative, preventing useful deeper search, or too permissive, collapsing back to vanilla behavior.

Reviewers may see the idea as related to iterative deepening; the paper must emphasize that the novelty is budget-aware staged expansion inside MCTS with adaptive evidence-based unlocking, not a standalone depth-limited search schedule.
If heuristic leaf evaluation is too strong, improvements may be attributed to better rollouts rather than depth staging itself, so careful rollout-matched ablations are necessary.
The proposal is primarily motivated by deterministic synthetic planning; generalization to stochastic or learned-model settings would remain future work.
